elements.sent-2411-001: Nuclear pairing is a well-established many-body correlation, particularly among like particles in a spin-singlet state.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the strength of spin-triplet proton--neutron (pn) pairing in nuclei has remained a long-standing and unresolved issue.
elements.sent-2411-003: The relative strength of spin-triplet pn pairing compared to spin-singlet one is investigated by introducing and analyzing the polarizability of the response to pn pair transfers.
elements.sent-2411-004: The nuclear energy-density functional method is employed.
elements.sent-2411-005: The ground state of the target nucleus is described using the Hartree--Fock--Bogoliubov approximation, which accounts for the conventional superfluidity of like-particle pairs.
elements.sent-2411-006: The response to pn pair transfers is then analyzed using the pn quasiparticle random-phase approximation.
elements.sent-2411-007: The spin-singlet pn-pair correlation is strongest at $N=Z$ and decreases monotonically with the increasing number of excess neutrons, whereas the spin-triplet pn-pair correlation is shown to depend non-monotonically on the neutron number and can be enhanced in cases where the pn-pair transfers involving the $\pi j_> \otimes \nu j_<$ configuration occur at low energy.
elements.sent-2411-008: The shell effect, which uniquely appears in spin-triplet pn-pair correlation, serves as a key indicator of the strength of pn-pair correlations.
elements.sent-2411-009: Pairing is a correlation occurring universally in assemblages made of interacting many particles, ranging from quark and nuclear to electron many-body systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: In infinite systems, the correlation is quantified by the introduction of an order parameter, specifically the pairing gap, which arises from condensation.
elements.sent-2411-011: When the correlation is sufficiently strong, fluctuations become significant, leading to a phase transition and the emergence of condensation.
elements.sent-2411-012: An analogy to superconductivity has been applied to finite nuclei by employing the Bardeen--Cooper--Schrieffer (BCS) theory~\cite{PhysRev.106.162 to the nuclear Hamiltonian~\cite{BM2,rin80 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Closed-shell nuclei are in a normal phase, where the gap function vanishes, while most nuclei exhibit a superconducting/superfluid phase at low energy.
elements.sent-2411-014: A non-vanishing pairing gap in the spin-singlet channel plays a central role in various low-energy nuclear phenomena, including the ground-state spin, staggering in the systematics of binding energies, low-lying quadrupole collective dynamics, rotational moments of inertia, and spontaneous fission half-lives~\cite{doi:10.1142/9789814412490_0003 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: A notable feature unique to finite systems is that pairing can be probed in response to a field changing the number of particles; the pair correlation leads to an enhanced two-nucleon transfer reaction~\cite{YOSHIDA1962685 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Heavy proton-rich nuclei along the $N=Z$ line have received a great deal of attention with the advancement of experimental techniques in rare-isotope beams for nuclear physics.
elements.sent-2411-017: Of particular interest is the possible appearance of exotic phenomena, attributed to the coherent shell effects of both protons and neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, the spin-triplet proton--neutron (pn) pair correlation is expected to be visible, as the spatial overlap between the proton and neutron single-particle wave functions would be large to form a Cooper pair of a proton and a neutron---a phenomenon initially predicted in light $N=Z$ nuclei~\cite{PhysRev.140.B26 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, the possible coexistence of the spin-singlet pn-pair condensation with the spin-triplet one is predicted~\cite{Goodman:1999zz, Gezerlis:2011rh .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Spin-orbit splitting is crucial in determining whether spin-triplet or spin-singlet pair correlation is stronger in medium-heavy nuclei~\cite{POVES1998203 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: If a nucleus is large enough that the surface spin-orbit potential can be neglected, the spin-triplet pn-pair superfluid appears in the ground state~\cite{Bertsch:2009xz , as is expected in symmetric nuclear matter~\cite{alm90, VONDERFECHT19911, BALDO19928, 10.1143/PTP.112.27 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, in reality, spin-orbit splitting suppresses spin-triplet pn-pair correlation in finite nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-023: Consequently, despite numerous attempts, the experimental evidence for spin-triplet pair condensation remains a topic of ongoing debate~\cite{Frauendorf:2014mja .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The present work further investigates the role of spin-orbit splitting to garner a deeper insight into the pn-pair correlations in medium-heavy nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-025: Previous studies have focused primarily on $N \approx Z$ nuclei, where the correlation between a proton and a neutron is the strongest.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, as demonstrated in Ref.~\cite{Gezerlis:2011rh , stepping away from the $N=Z$ line can also reveal unexpected and novel findings.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this study, I explore the pn-pair correlations across a wide mass region from calcium to tin isotopes, extending from the $N=Z$ line to the neutron-rich side.
elements.sent-2411-028: To systematically study the pn-pair correlations, I employ the notion of the fluctuation of order parameters.
elements.sent-2411-029: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-030: In Sec.~\ref{Sec.Th , I rehash the static polarizability/susceptibility in response to an external field and then extend it to the case for the response to pn-pair transfers.}
elements.sent-2411-031: A numerical method I employ is briefly summarized in Sec.~\ref{Sec.numer .}
elements.sent-2411-032: In Sec.~\ref{Sec.Result , I show the results and discuss the roles of excess neutrons on the pn-pair polarizabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To explore the collectivity, one may perform the constrained Hartree--Fock--Bogoliubov (HFB) calculations in the presence of an external field $\lambda \hat{F}$ relevant to the collective mode, i.e. one solves \begin{equation}\delta \bra{\phi(\lambda)}\hat{H}-\lambda \hat{F}\ket{\phi(\lambda)}=0,\end{equation} where $\hat{H}$ is the Hamiltonian and $\ket{\phi}$ the HFB state vector.
elements.sent-2411-035: The static polarizability $\alpha$ is defined by the change of the expectation value of $\hat{F}$~\cite{rin80 : \begin{equation}\bra{\phi(\lambda)}\hat{F}\ket{\phi(\lambda)}=\bra{\phi_0}\hat{F}\ket{\phi_0}+\lambda \alpha\end{equation} and the change in energy is given by \begin{equation}\bra{\phi(\lambda)}\hat{H}\ket{\phi(\lambda)}=\bra{\phi_0}\hat{H}\ket{\phi_0}+\dfrac{1}{2}\lambda^2 \alpha,\end{equation} where $\ket{\phi_0 =\ket{\phi(0)}$ is the solution of the HFB equation.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: One then evaluates the static polarizability, the radius of curvature of the potential energy surface at the HFB equilibrium, as \begin{equation}\alpha=\dfrac{d^2}{d\lambda^2}\bra{\phi(\lambda)}\hat{H}\ket{\phi(\lambda)}|_{\lambda=0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: Alternatively, one can compute the response functions $R_F(\omega)$ of the system to the external field $\hat{F}$ in the random-phase approximation (RPA): \begin{equation}R_F(\omega)=\sum_n\left(\dfrac{|\bra{n} \hat{F} \ket{0}|^2}{\omega-\omega_n+i\eta } -\dfrac{|\bra{0} \hat{F} \ket{n}|^2}{\omega+\omega_n+i\eta }\right),\end{equation} where $\ket{n}$ and $\omega_n$ denote the RPA eigenstates and eigenenergies.
elements.sent-2411-038: If one is interested in the static polarizability, it is sufficient to calculate the inversely-energy weighted sum (IEWS) of the strength distribution: \begin{equation}\alpha=-R_F(0)=2m_{-1}(F),\end{equation} with $m_k(F)$ being the $k$-th moment of the strength distribution.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is nothing but the dielectric theorem~\cite{Capelli:2009zz .}
elements.sent-2411-040: It is noted that the IEWS and the static polarizability have been discussed intensively for the case of the $E1$ strength function, i.e. the electric dipole excitation in nuclei, as a probe of neutron skin and nuclear matter properties~\cite{PhysRevLett.107.062502, Piekarewicz:2012pp,Roca-Maza:2018ujj .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Quite recently, the pair collectivity has been studied in terms of the pair polarizability~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The pair field with spin $S$ and isospin $T$ is defined as \begin{equation}\hat{P}_{ST}(r) := \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\sum_{s s'}\sum_{t t'} \hat{\psi}(r\tilde{s}' \tilde{t}') \hat{\psi}(rs t) S_{S \mu_\sigma, s' s} T_{T \mu_\tau, t' t} \label{pair_field}\end{equation} with the nucleon field operator $\hat{\psi (\br s t)$, and $\hat{\psi (\boldsymbol{r \tilde{s \tilde{t ) := (-2s)(-2t)\hat{\psi (\boldsymbol{r -s-t)$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\hat{P}_{ST}(r) := \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\sum_{s s'}\sum_{t t'} \hat{\psi}(r\tilde{s}' \tilde{t}') \hat{\psi}(rs t) S_{S \mu_\sigma, s' s} T_{T \mu_\tau, t' t} \label{pair_field}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: The spin and isospin operators are defined as \begin{equation}S_{00}=1, S_{1\mu_\sigma}=\sigma_{\mu_\sigma}, T_{00}=1, T_{1\mu_\tau}=\tau_{\mu_\tau}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: Here, $\sigma_{\mu_\sigma}$ and $\tau_{\mu_\tau}$ are the spherical components of spin and isospin Pauli matrices, respectively; $s=\pm 1/2$ stands for the $z$-component of spin; $t=\pm 1/2$ stands for neutron and proton.
elements.sent-2411-045: The factor of $1/\sqrt{2}$ is put here so that the two-neutron pair field ($\mu_\tau=1$) coincides with the pair field operator given in Ref.~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Note that it is convenient to introduce the factor of $1/\sqrt{2}$ when investigating the isovector modes of excitation with $T_z=\pm1$ and $T_z=0$ simultaneously~\cite{Yoshida:2021gla .}
elements.sent-2411-047: One can expect the pn-pair correlations to be probed by the response of the pn-pair transfers~\cite{FROBRICH1971338, PhysRevLett.94.162502 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: I then introduce an $L=0$ pn-pair addition operator \begin{equation}\hat{P}^{{\rm ad}}_{ST} :=\int drY_{00}(\hat{r})f(r)\hat{P}^\dagger_{ST}(r) \label{eq:pair_ad}\end{equation} and an $L=0$ pn-pair removal operator \begin{equation}\hat{P}^{{\rm rm}}_{ST} :=\int drY_{00}(\hat{r})f(r)\hat{P}_{ST}(r), \label{eq:pair_rem}\end{equation} with $\mu_\tau=0$ for $T=1$.
elements.sent-2411-049: These operators bring about a transition, which changes the particle number $\Delta N= \pm 2$ with $\Delta T_z=0$.
elements.sent-2411-050: As in the study of a two-neutron transfer~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn , I introduced a form factor $f(r)$, which is effective in a spatial region where the nucleon density is finite, but vanishes far outside the nucleus, as I am interested in a process where a pn pair is added to or removed from a nucleus.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Specifically, I choose a Woods--Saxon function \begin{equation}f(r)=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{(r-R)/a}} \label{eq:WS_form}\end{equation} with $R = 1.27 \times A^{1/3 \,{\rm fm}$ and $a = 0.67 \,{\rm fm}$, but as shown later the main conclusion does not depend on a detailed form of $f(r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Here, $Y_{00}$ is a spherical harmonics with rank 0 and both $\hat{P ^{\rm ad}$ and $\hat{P ^{\rm rm}$ carry the angular and parity quantum numbers $0^+$.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: I describe the pn-pair vibration with spin--parity $J^\pi = 0^+$ and $1^+$ for $(S,T)=(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-054: I investigate the static polarizability as a measure of the pn pairing.
elements.sent-2411-055: One may consider the polarizability for the hermitian operators $\hat{P ^{\rm ad +\hat{P ^{\rm rm}$ and $i(\hat{P ^{\rm ad -\hat{P ^{\rm rm )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: They indeed correspond to the operators for the amplitude and phase modes, respectively~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, the order parameter of the pn pairing is $ \langle \phi_0|\hat{P ^{\rm A _{ST |\phi_0 \rangle $ with $\hat{P ^{\rm A _{ST =\hat{P ^{\rm{ad _{ST +\hat{P ^{\rm rm _{ST}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The polarizability appropriate for the pair collectivity is then evaluated as \begin{equation}\alpha_{ST}=2\sum_{n}\dfrac{|\langle n|\hat{P}^{\rm{A}}_{ST}|0\rangle|^2}{\omega_n},\end{equation} which is an extension of the two-neutron pair polarizability in Ref.~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn to the case for the pn pair.}
elements.sent-2411-059: I assume in the present investigation that the condensation of the pn pairs ($T_z=0$) does not occur in the ground state: $\langle \phi_0|\hat{P ^{\rm ad _{ST |\phi_0\rangle = \langle \phi_0|\hat{P ^{\rm rm _{ST |\phi_0\rangle=0$ for both $(S,T)=(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this case, the pn-pair polarizability reads \begin{equation}\alpha_{ST}=2\sum_{n}\left(\dfrac{|\langle n|\hat{P}^{\rm{ad}}_{ST}|0\rangle|^2}{\omega_n}+\dfrac{|\langle n|\hat{P}^{\rm{rm}}_{ST}|0\rangle|^2}{\omega_n}\right). \label{eq:pair_pol}\end{equation} If the system tends to break the symmetry, one sees the polarizability gets diverged, or one obtains the imaginary solution of the RPA equation.
elements.sent-2411-061: The static polarizability depends on the form of an operator.
elements.sent-2411-062: The reaction mechanism also enters into the form factor.
elements.sent-2411-063: In envisioning the pn-transfer reactions, such as $(p,{ ^{3 {\rm He )$ and $({ ^{3 {\rm He ,p)$, in which the isoscalar (IS) and isovector (IV) transfers are both allowed, what is proposed here is the ratio of the polarizabilities of the pn pair with $(S,T)=(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$: \begin{equation}R_{01}:=3\dfrac{\alpha_{01}}{\alpha_{10}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: A factor of three is introduced here to cancel out the spin degeneracy $2S+1$.
elements.sent-2411-065: When the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value is greater than unity, the $S=0, T=1$ pn pairing is dominant over the $S=1, T=0$ pn pairing.}
elements.sent-2411-066: As in the ratio of the pn-transfer cross sections from the ground state of an even-even nucleus to the $0_1^+$ and $1_1^+$ states in an odd-odd nucleus, one can expect systematic uncertainties and, to some extent, kinematic conditions of the reaction cancel out~\cite{LAY2022136789 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: As a numerical approach for the present investigation, I employ the nuclear energy-density functional (EDF) method.
elements.sent-2411-068: Since the details can be found in Ref.~\cite{Yoshida:2013bma , here I briefly recapitulate the basic equations relevant to the present study.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The ground state of a mother (target) nucleus is described by solving the HFB or Kohn--Sham--Bogoliubov (KSB) equation~\cite{dob84 : \begin{equation}\sum_{s^\prime}  h^q_{s s^\prime}(\br)-\lambda^{q}\delta_{s s^\prime} \tilde{h}^q_{s s^\prime}(\br) \tilde{h}^q_{s s^\prime}(\br) -h^q_{s s^\prime}(\br)+\lambda^q\delta_{s s^\prime}   \varphi^{q}_{1,\alpha}(\br s^\prime) \varphi^{q}_{2,\alpha}(\br s^\prime)  \notag = E_{\alpha}  \varphi^{q}_{1,\alpha}(\br s) \varphi^{q}_{2,\alpha}(\br s) , \label{HFB_eq}\end{equation} where the single-particle and pair Hamiltonians, $h^q_{s s^\prime (\br)$ and $\tilde{h ^q_{s s^\prime (\br)$, are given by the functional derivative of the EDF with respect to the particle density and the pair density, respectively.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\sum_{s^\prime}  h^q_{s s^\prime}(\br)-\lambda^{q}\delta_{s s^\prime} \tilde{h}^q_{s s^\prime}(\br) \tilde{h}^q_{s s^\prime}(\br) -h^q_{s s^\prime}(\br)+\lambda^q\delta_{s s^\prime}   \varphi^{q}_{1,\alpha}(\br s^\prime) \varphi^{q}_{2,\alpha}(\br s^\prime)  \notag = E_{\alpha}  \varphi^{q}_{1,\alpha}(\br s) \varphi^{q}_{2,\alpha}(\br s) , \label{HFB_eq}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: An explicit expression of the Hamiltonians is found in the Appendix of Ref.~\cite{kas21 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: The average particle number is fixed at the desired value by adjusting the chemical potential $\lambda^q$.
elements.sent-2411-073: When the system is in the normal phase, the chemical potential is defined by averaging the highest occupied single-particle energy and the lowest unoccupied single-particle energy.
elements.sent-2411-074: Assuming the system is axially symmetric, the KSB equation (\ref{HFB_eq ) is block diagonalized according to the quantum number $\Omega$, the $z$-component of the total angular momentum.}
elements.sent-2411-075: As mentioned in the above, I further assumed that the condensation of the pn pairs ($T_z=0$) does not occur but that of the like-particle pairs ($T_z=\pm 1$) can happen in the ground state.
elements.sent-2411-076: I solve the KSB equation in the coordinate space using cylindrical coordinates $\boldsymbol{r =(\varrho,z,\phi)$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Since I assume further the reflection symmetry, only the region of $z\geq 0$ is considered.
elements.sent-2411-078: I use a two-dimensional lattice mesh with $\varrho_i=(i-1/2)h$, $z_j=(j-1)h$ ($i,j=1,2,\dots$) with a mesh size of $h=0.6$ fm and 25 points for each direction.
elements.sent-2411-079: The qp states are truncated according to the qp energy cutoff at 60 MeV, and the qp states up to the magnetic quantum number $\Omega=31/2$ with positive and negative parities are included.
elements.sent-2411-080: The excited states in a neighboring odd-odd nucleus $| n \rangle$ are described as one-phonon excitations built on the ground state $|0\rangle$ of the even-even mother nucleus as \begin{equation}| n \rangle = \hat{\Gamma}^\dagger_n |0 \rangle, \hat{\Gamma}^\dagger_n = \sum_{\alpha \beta}\left\{ X_{\alpha \beta}^n \hat{a}^\dagger_{\alpha}\hat{a}^\dagger_{\beta} -Y_{\alpha \beta}^n \hat{a}_{\bar{\beta}}\hat{a}_{\bar{\alpha}}\right\},\end{equation} where $\hat{a ^\dagger$ and $\hat{a}$ are the quasiparticle (qp) creation and annihilation operators that are defined in terms of the solutions of the KSB equation (\ref{HFB_eq ) with the Bogoliubov transformation.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The phonon states, the amplitudes $X^n, Y^n$ and the vibrational frequency $\omega_n$, are obtained in the quasiparticle-RPA (QRPA): the linearized time-dependent density-functional theory for superfluid systems~\cite{nak16 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The EDF gives the residual interactions entering into the QRPA equation.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the axially symmetric nuclei, the QRPA equation is block diagonalized according to the quantum number $K=\Omega_\alpha + \Omega_\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-084: In solving the QRPA equation, I introduce the truncation for the two-quasiparticle (2qp) configurations, in terms of the 2qp-energy as 60 MeV.
elements.sent-2411-085: The calculated energy and transition strength of the low-lying and giant resonance states are almost converged with respect to the mesh size, the box size, and the energy cutoff~\cite{yos08 , and are compatible with the results obtained in different methodology~\cite{eba10,sca14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: For the normal (particle--hole) part of the EDF $\mathcal{E _{\rm ph}$, I employ the Skyrme-type SGII functional~\cite{gia81 .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For the pairing energy $\mathcal{E _{\rm pair}$, I adopt the one in Ref.~\cite{yam09 that depends on both the IS and IV densities, in addition to the pair density, with the parameters given in Table~III of Ref.~\cite{yam09 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The same pair interaction is employed for the dynamical pairing for the $S=0$ and $S=1$ pn pairs in the QRPA calculation, while the linear term in the IV density is dropped.
elements.sent-2411-089: I multiply a factor $f$ to the pairing EDF for $S=1$: $\mathcal{E _{\rm pair ^{S=1 =f\times \mathcal{E _{\rm pair ^{S=0}$ to study the roles of the dynamic spin-triplet pairing.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Note that the QRPA calculations including the dynamic spin-triplet pairing with more or less the same strength as the spin-singlet pairing $(f \sim 1)$ describe well the characteristic low-lying Gamow--Teller strength distributions in the light $N \simeq Z$ nuclei~\cite{fuj14,fuj15,fuj19 , and the $\beta$-decay half-lives of neutron-rich Ni isotopes~\cite{yos19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Figure~\ref{fig:40Ca_pair_strength shows the calculated strength functions for the pair-addition and removal processes in $^{40}$Ca.}
elements.sent-2411-092: A possible appearance of the collective states in the pn-pair transfer from ${ ^{40}$Ca was discussed in Ref.~\cite{Yoshida:2014jga .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: However, only the lowest $1^+$ state in the odd-odd neighboring nuclei, $^{42 {\rm Sc}$ and $^{38 {\rm K}$, was investigated as a collective state of the $S=1$ pn pairing.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{fig1.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:40Ca_pair_strength} Calculated pair addition and removal strengths in $^{40}$Ca smeared by a Lorentzian function with a width parameter of 0.5 MeV. (a): $S=0$ pn-pair strengths. (b): Unperturbed $S=0$ pn-pair strengths. (c): $S=1$ pn-pair strengths with $f=1.0$. (d): Unperturbed $S=1$ pn-pair strengths. }
elements.sent-2411-094: As is observed in the figure, not only the lowest state but also the higher-lying states acquire appreciable strengths for both the $S=0$ and $S=1$ pn-pair transfers.
elements.sent-2411-095:
The occurrence of the higher-lying states is due to the shell structure: The second $0^+$ state seen in the pn-pair addition is mainly generated by the $
u p_{3/2}\otimes
u p_{3/2}$ configuration with an admixture of $
u p_{1/2}\otimes
u p_{1/2}$; while the second $1^+$ state is constructed by the $
u p_{3/2}\otimes
u p_{1/2}$ and $
u p_{1/2}\otimes
u p_{3/2}$ configurations additionally.
elements.sent-2411-096: One can see there are several states even above 20 MeV.
elements.sent-2411-097: These states may also be regarded as pair-vibrational states as the excitation energies are lowered and the pair-transfer strengths are enhanced by the RPA correlation, as compared with the unperturbed strength functions depicted in Figs.~\ref{fig:40Ca_pair_strength (b) and \ref{fig:40Ca_pair_strength (d).}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Although high-lying pair vibration is often called the giant pair vibration~\cite{BROGLIA1977129,cap15, ass19, cav19 , I adopt in the present paper, following Ref.~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn , the more inclusive term ``high-lying pair vibrations'' for these peaks since the strength distribution does not form a single resonance peak but rather multiple peaks in a wide energy interval.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.45]{fig2.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:40Ca_sum} The inversely-energy-weighted sum of the pn-pair addition and removal strength functions for $^{40}$Ca, as a function of the maximum energy $E_{\rm max}$ of the sum: $\omega_n<E_{\rm max}$. }
elements.sent-2411-099: The primal motivation to introduce the pair polarizability in the present study is to relate the pair vibrations distributed in a broad energy region with the macroscopic picture as an amplitude oscillation of the order parameter.
elements.sent-2411-100: The polarizability is defined by the inversely-energy-weight sum of the strength functions, as given in Eq.~(\ref{eq:pair_pol ).}
elements.sent-2411-101: The low-lying pair vibrational states make a substantial contribution to polarizability.
elements.sent-2411-102: The high-lying pair vibrations can additionally play a role.
elements.sent-2411-103: Figure~\ref{fig:40Ca_sum shows a running sum in Eq.~(\ref{eq:pair_pol ) up to $E_{\rm max}$, where the upper bound $E_{\rm max}$ is varied for $^{40}$Ca with $f=1.0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The low-lying states below 15 MeV are dominant for the polarizability, and more than 90\% of the polarizability is given by the states up to 25 MeV.
elements.sent-2411-105: This is similar to the finding in Ref.~\cite{Takahashi:2023rfn .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{fig3.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:Ca_pair_pol} (a): Polarizalibities for the pn-pair transfers with $S=0$ and $S=1$ for the Ca isotopes. For those with $S=1$, the results obtained by changing $f$ are also shown. (b): The ratio $\mathcal{R}_{01}$ calculated by changing $f$. The results obtained by using the surface-type form factor are also shown for the case of $f=1.3$ (Surf). }
elements.sent-2411-106: Figure~\ref{fig:Ca_pair_pol (a) shows the polarizabilities for pn-pair transfers with $(S,T)=(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The $S=1$ pn-pair polarizability has a peak at $N=Z$ and drops with an increase in the neutron number.
elements.sent-2411-108: This is what one would expect: the pn-pair correlation is strongest at $N=Z$ and gets weaker with the neutron excess.
elements.sent-2411-109: Without the $S=1$ pn-pair interaction ($f=0$), the $S=1$ pn-pair polarizability keeps constant at a low value.
elements.sent-2411-110: When considering the pn-pair interaction in the $S=1$ channel with $f>0$, the polarizability shows maximal at $N=Z$ and decreases with increasing the neutron number.
elements.sent-2411-111: However, one sees the persistence around $N=30$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Beyond ${ ^{48}$Ca, the $\nu f_{5/2}$ orbital is located near the Fermi level, and then the $S=1$ pairs of the $\pi f_{7/2}\otimes \nu f_{5/2}$ configuration are created with low energy.}
elements.sent-2411-113: It would not be easy to obtain the absolute values of the pair polarizability since the reaction condition is involved.
elements.sent-2411-114: Instead, the isotopic dependence of the pair polarizability of $S=0$ and $S=1$ provides us with information on pair collectivity, as the shell effect uniquely appears in the $S=1$ pn-pair transfer.
elements.sent-2411-115: Alternatively, by taking the ratio of the pair polarizabilities of $S=0$ and $S=1$, one can gain insight into the pairing collectivity.

elements.sent-2411-117: Without the pn-pair interaction in the $S=1$ channel, it is evident that the $S=0$ pairing is dominant in $N \approx Z$ isotopes: the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value is far above the unity.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Even with a reasonable strength ($f=1.0$) of the $S=1$ pn pair interaction, the $S=0$ pairing is stronger than the $S=1$ pairing around $N=Z$.
elements.sent-2411-119: However, the $S=0$ and $S=1$ pair correlations vie in collectivity around ${ ^{50}$Ca.}
elements.sent-2411-120: In the calculation with $f=1.3$, the lowest $1^+$ state is lower than the $0^+$ state by 0.6 (0.2) MeV for the pn-pair addition (removal) transfer, and the transition strength to the $1^+$ state is strongly enhanced for $^{40}$Ca.
elements.sent-2411-121: It seems that the system is close to the critical point of the $S=1$ pn-pair condensation; the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value is about 0.5.}
elements.sent-2411-122: In reality, however, the $S=1$ pn-pairing is not so strong because the $1^+$ state is higher in energy than the $0^+$ state in $^{42}$Sc and $^{38}$K.
elements.sent-2411-123: The method to evaluate the pn-pair collectivity, the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value, from the response should not depend on details of the definitions of the pn-pair transfer operators, $\hat{P ^{\rm ad , \hat{P ^{\rm rm , \hat{P ^{\rm A}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: I have examined another choice of the form factor $f(r)$ appearing in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:pair_ad ) and (\ref{eq:pair_rem ) by using $f(r)=(d/dr)[1+e^{(r-R)/a ]^{-1}$, which has a weight on the surface area of the nucleus.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The results obtained by using this surface-type form factor are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Ca_pair_pol (b) for the case of $f=1.3$, in which the amplitude of the transition density is high and thus the results may be sensitive to the spatial dependence of the operator.}
elements.sent-2411-126: As one can see in the figure, the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value obtained is almost the same as that obtained by the volume-type form factor.}
elements.sent-2411-127: I can conclude that the $\mathcal{R _{01}$ value does not depend on the choice of the form factor.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=0.29]{fig4a.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.29]{fig4b.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:Ni_Sn_pair_pol} As Fig.~\ref{fig:Ca_pair_pol} but for the Ni and Sn isotopes. }
elements.sent-2411-128: The shell effect in the $S=1$ pn-pairing is further investigated for the Ni and Sn isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-129: The calculated pn-pair polarizabilities $\alpha_{01 , \alpha_{10}$ and the ratio $\mathcal{R _{01}$ are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Ni_Sn_pair_pol .}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: As in the case of the Ca isotopes, the pn-pair polarizabilities are large at $N \approx Z$ for both $S=0$ and $S=1$ with inclusion of the spin-triplet pair interaction ($f>0$).
elements.sent-2411-131: However, the $S=1$ pn-pair polarizability $\alpha_{10}$ increases toward $N=40$ in the Ni isotopes and keeps almost constant up to $N=64$ in the Sn isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-132: This isotopic dependence is clearly seen in the ratio $\mathcal{R _{01}$: one sees a minimum at $N=40$ and 64 in the Ni and Sn isotope, respectively; the $S=1$ pn-pair correlation is stronger than the $S=0$ one.}
elements.sent-2411-133: The shell effect at $N=40$ and 64 is not due to the appearance of the $S=1$ pn-pair addition mode, as seen in the Ca isotopes, but due to that of the $S=1$ pn-pair removal mode in low energy.
elements.sent-2411-134: Figure~\ref{fig:Ni_Sn_IEWS shows the IEWS of the pn-pair addition and removal strengths.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The IEWS for the $S=1$ pn-pair removal increases toward $N=40$ and 64 in the Ni and Sn isotopes, respectively, while the IEWSs for both the $S=0$ pn-pair addition and removal as well as the $S=1$ pn-pair addition decrease monotonically as stepping away from $N=Z$.
elements.sent-2411-136: It is noted that the IWSEs for the $S=1$ pn-pair addition and removal keep almost constant as functions of the neutron number when the spin-triplet pair interaction is discarded, as expected from the calculated pair polarizability $\alpha_{10}$ with $f=0$; the enhancement in the IWSE for the $S=1$ pn-pair removal is much more significant in the calculations with $f>1$.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[scale=0.45]{fig5.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:Ni_Sn_IEWS} IEWS of the pn-pair addition and removal strengths in (a) Ni and (b) Sn isotopes, calculated by using $f=1.0$ for the spin-triplet pair interaction. }
elements.sent-2411-137: A decisive role in the shell effect uniquely appearing in the $S=1$ pn-pairing in the medium-heavy nuclei such as in the Ni and Sn isotopes is played by the occupation of the intruder proton $j_>$ orbital.
elements.sent-2411-138: Indeed, this diminishes the $S=1$ pn-pairing in the $N \approx Z$ nuclei beyond $^{40}$Ca: only either the $\pi j_> \otimes \nu j_>$ or $\pi j_< \otimes \nu j_<$ configuration is devoted to the pairing.
elements.sent-2411-139: With an increase in the neutron number from $N=Z$, the Fermi level of neutrons approaches the $j_<$ orbital.
elements.sent-2411-140: As neutrons start to occupy the $j_<$ orbital, the pair of the $\pi j_> \otimes \nu j_<$ configuration, which is exclusively in $S=1$, appears to contribute to the pairing collectivity.
elements.sent-2411-141: In the present case, the pair of the $\pi f_{7/2}\otimes \nu f_{5/2}$ and $\pi g_{9/2 \otimes \nu g_{7/2}$ configuration gives a significant contribution to the $S=1$ pairing in the Ni and Sn isotopes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Proton-neutron (pn) pair correlations have been investigated in the Ca, Ni, and Sn isotopes as examples of medium-heavy nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-143: The collectivity was quantified by introducing the polarizability for the response to pn-pair transfers.
elements.sent-2411-144: The polarizabilities were evaluated in the framework of the nuclear energy-density functional method.
elements.sent-2411-145: The spin-singlet pn-pair correlation is the strongest at $N=Z$ and decreases monotonically as the neutron number increases.
elements.sent-2411-146: In contrast, the spin-triplet pn-pair correlation is shown to depend non-monotonically on the neutron number and can be enhanced in cases where the pn-pair removal modes involving the $\pi j_> \otimes \nu j_<$ configuration occur at low energy.
elements.sent-2411-147: Therefore, the relative strength of spin-triplet and spin-singlet pn-pair correlations becomes large in the neutron-rich isotopes when neutrons occupy the $j_<$ orbital.
